title: 88.	Minimum path visiting all nodes (graph state DP).
description: 88.	Minimum path visiting all nodes (graph state DP).

examples.explanation: n=3 nodes. Start from node 0: visited=001 (binary). DFS(0,001,0): unvisited nodes: 1,2. Try node 1: dist=graph[0][1]=1, visited=011, total=1. DFS(1,011,1): unvisited: 2. Try node 2: dist=graph[1][2]=3, visited=111, total=4. All visited, update min=4. Backtrack to node 0: try node 2: dist=graph[0][2]=2, visited=101, total=2. DFS(2,101,2): unvisited: 1. Try node 1: dist=graph[2][1]=3, visited=111, total=5. Update min=4. Backtrack. Start from node 1: visited=010. DFS(1,010,0): unvisited: 0,2. Try 0: dist=1, visited=011, total=1. DFS(0,011,1): unvisited: 2. Try 2: dist=2, visited=111, total=3. Update min=3. Try 2 first: dist=3, visited=110, total=3. DFS(2,110,3): unvisited: 0. Try 0: dist=2, visited=111, total=5. Min remains 3. Start from node 2: similar, min=3. Final min=3.

n=2 nodes. Start from node 0: visited=01. DFS(0,01,0): unvisited: 1. Try node 1: dist=5, visited=11, total=5. Update min=5. Start from node 1: visited=10. DFS(1,10,0): unvisited: 0. Try node 0: dist=5, visited=11, total=5. Min=5.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(n!)
approaches.summary: Generate all permutations of node visits using DFS to explore every possible path from each starting node, computing the total distance for each permutation. Use this for small graphs (n <= 10) to understand the problem, but it's impractical for larger n due to factorial growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DFS for Minimum Path Visiting All Nodes
ladders.desc: Generate all permutations of node visits using DFS to find the shortest path covering all nodes in a weighted graph.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute-force solution to the problem of finding the minimum path length to visit all nodes in a weighted graph exactly once (or with possible revisits, but here we're finding Hamiltonian path approximations). The intuition is that for small graphs (n ≤ 10), we can exhaustively explore every possible ordering of nodes to find the optimal path. We treat this as searching through all permutations of nodes, which is n! possibilities. We use DFS to generate these permutations recursively, starting from each node as the potential starting point. At each step, we add an unvisited node to the current path, compute the cumulative distance using the graph's adjacency matrix, and backtrack to explore other possibilities. We maintain a global minimum to track the shortest total distance across all complete paths. The algorithm works because it systematically explores the entire solution space—every possible sequence of visiting nodes—ensuring we don't miss the optimal path. However, its factorial time complexity makes it impractical for n > 10. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize min_distance = infinity. 2. For each node as start: call DFS(start, visited_mask, current_distance). 3. In DFS: if all nodes visited (mask == (1<<n)-1), update min_distance. 4. Else: for each unvisited node next: compute distance from current to next via adjacency matrix, mark next visited, recurse, then backtrack. 5. Return min_distance. Time: O(n! * n) due to n! permutations and O(n) work per permutation to compute distances. Space: O(n) for recursion depth and visited mask. Connection to L1: This uses the DFS permutation generator from L1 as its core subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling graphs with no direct edges (use large distance for unreachable), forgetting to backtrack visited nodes, or missing the case where all nodes are visited. Real code pattern: def min_path_brute_force(graph): n = len(graph); min_dist = float('inf'); for start in range(n): visited = 1 << start; dfs(start, visited, 0); return min_dist.

examples.trace: n=3 nodes. Start from node 0: visited=001 (binary). DFS(0,001,0): unvisited nodes: 1,2. Try node 1: dist=graph[0][1]=1, visited=011, total=1. DFS(1,011,1): unvisited: 2. Try node 2: dist=graph[1][2]=3, visited=111, total=4. All visited, update min=4. Backtrack to node 0: try node 2: dist=graph[0][2]=2, visited=101, total=2. DFS(2,101,2): unvisited: 1. Try node 1: dist=graph[2][1]=3, visited=111, total=5. Update min=4. Backtrack. Start from node 1: visited=010. DFS(1,010,0): unvisited: 0,2. Try 0: dist=1, visited=011, total=1. DFS(0,011,1): unvisited: 2. Try 2: dist=2, visited=111, total=3. Update min=3. Try 2 first: dist=3, visited=110, total=3. DFS(2,110,3): unvisited: 0. Try 0: dist=2, visited=111, total=5. Min remains 3. Start from node 2: similar, min=3. Final min=3.

n=2 nodes. Start from node 0: visited=01. DFS(0,01,0): unvisited: 1. Try node 1: dist=5, visited=11, total=5. Update min=5. Start from node 1: visited=10. DFS(1,10,0): unvisited: 0. Try node 0: dist=5, visited=11, total=5. Min=5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Permutation Generator for Node Visits
ladders.desc: Generate all permutations of node visits using DFS and backtracking.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute-force approach: it recursively generates all possible orders (permutations) in which nodes can be visited. The intuition is that to explore every possible path, we need to consider every sequence of nodes, which corresponds to all permutations of the node indices. We use DFS with backtracking to build these permutations incrementally. Starting from a current node and a set of visited nodes (represented as a bitmask for efficiency), we try adding each unvisited node to the path, recurse to extend the path further, and then backtrack to try other nodes. This ensures we generate all n! permutations when starting from a fixed node. Step-by-step: 1. Base case: if all nodes visited (mask == (1<<n)-1), we have a complete permutation. 2. Recursive case: for each node i not in visited mask: mark i as visited, add i to current path, recurse with updated mask and path, then unmark i (backtrack). Time: O(n!) to generate all permutations from a start node. Space: O(n) for recursion stack and path storage. Connection to L0: This generator is called repeatedly in L0 for each starting node to build full paths and compute distances. Common mistakes: Not resetting the visited mask after recursion (backtracking), or incorrect base case condition. Real code pattern: def dfs_permute(current, visited, path): if visited == all_visited: process(path); return; for next_node in range(n): if not (visited >> next_node) & 1: dfs_permute(next_node, visited | (1<<next_node), path + [next_node]).

examples.trace: Start: current=0, visited=001, path=[0]. Unvisited: 1,2. Try 1: visited=011, path=[0,1]. Unvisited: 2. Try 2: visited=111, path=[0,1,2] -> complete permutation. Backtrack to [0,1]: visited=011. Backtrack to [0]: visited=001. Try 2: visited=101, path=[0,2]. Unvisited: 1. Try 1: visited=111, path=[0,2,1] -> complete permutation.
Start: current=1, visited=10, path=[1]. Unvisited: 0. Try 0: visited=11, path=[1,0] -> complete permutation.

testCases.input: n=1, start=0, but with visited initially including 0

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking to Explore All Paths
ladders.desc: Use backtracking in DFS to systematically explore and retreat from partial paths.
ladders.explanation: Backtracking is the algorithmic technique that allows us to explore all possible solutions by building candidates incrementally and abandoning a candidate (backtracking) as soon as it's determined not to lead to a valid solution. In this problem, we use it to generate all permutations of node visits. The intuition is that we construct a path step by step: at each step, we add a new unvisited node to the current path, then recursively extend it. If we reach a complete path (all nodes visited), we process it. After returning from recursion, we remove the last node (backtrack) to try a different node at that position. This ensures we explore all combinations without repetition. Step-by-step: 1. Choose: select an unvisited node to add to the path. 2. Explore: recurse to extend the path further. 3. Unchoose: remove the node (backtrack) to restore state for other choices. Time: O(branching factor^depth) = O(n!) in worst case. Space: O(n) for recursion depth. Connection to L1: This backtracking mechanism is implemented within the DFS permutation generator. Common mistakes: Forgetting to unchoose (leading to incorrect state), or not pruning early when possible (though here we don't prune). Real code pattern: path.append(node); backtrack(visited, path); path.pop().

examples.trace: Start: path=[A], unvisited={B,C}. Choose B: path=[A,B], unvisited={C}. Explore: from [A,B], choose C: path=[A,B,C], unvisited={} -> complete. Backtrack: remove C, path=[A,B], unvisited={C}. Backtrack further: remove B, path=[A], unvisited={B,C}. Choose C: path=[A,C], unvisited={B}. Explore: choose B: path=[A,C,B], unvisited={} -> complete.
Start: path=[X], unvisited={Y}. Choose Y: path=[X,Y], unvisited={} -> complete. Backtrack: remove Y, path=[X].

testCases.input: path=[X], unvisited={Y,Z}, but Y is invalid due to constraints (none here)

ladders.title: Building Block — Bitmask for Visited Nodes
ladders.desc: Use bitmasking to efficiently track visited nodes in a set.
ladders.explanation: Bitmasking is a technique where we use an integer's bits to represent a set of items—here, nodes in a graph. Each bit corresponds to a node: bit i is 1 if node i is visited, 0 otherwise. For n nodes, we use an n-bit mask (typically stored in an integer, feasible for n up to 20-30 in practice). The intuition is that this provides O(1) time for set operations (add, remove, check membership) and reduces space compared to a boolean array or set. It's crucial for the DFS backtracking because we need to quickly check which nodes are unvisited and update the visited set as we recurse. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize mask = 0 (no nodes visited). 2. To mark node i visited: mask = mask | (1 << i). 3. To check if node i is visited: (mask >> i) & 1. 4. To check if all n nodes visited: mask == (1 << n) - 1. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1) for the integer. Connection to L2: This bitmask is used in the backtracking logic to manage the visited set efficiently. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in bit shifts, not using parentheses in bit operations, or assuming unlimited bits (use integers with enough bits). Real code pattern: visited = 0; visited |= 1 << node; if (visited >> node) & 1: ...; all_visited = (1 << n) - 1.

examples.trace: Start mask=0 (000). Mark node 0: 1<<0=001, mask=0|001=001. Mark node 2: 1<<2=100, mask=001|100=101 binary, which is 5 decimal. Check node 1: (101 >> 1) & 1 = (010) & 1 = 0 -> not visited. All visited? (1<<3)-1 = 111 = 7, mask=5 ≠ 7 -> not all.
Start mask=0 (00). Mark node 1: 1<<1=10, mask=0|10=10 binary (2 decimal). Check node 0: (10 >> 0) & 1 = (10) & 1 = 0 -> not visited. All visited? (1<<2)-1=11=3, mask=2 ≠ 3.

testCases.input: n=3, check if node 2 is visited in mask=101 binary
n=4, check if all visited for mask=1111 binary
n=3, mark node 1 visited in mask=001 binary

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Graph Representation as Adjacency Matrix
ladders.desc: Represent a weighted graph using a 2D adjacency matrix.
ladders.explanation: An adjacency matrix is a square matrix used to represent a finite graph. The element at row i and column j represents the weight of the edge from node i to node j. For an undirected graph, the matrix is symmetric (graph[i][j] = graph[j][i]). If there's no edge, we use a large value (e.g., infinity) to indicate unreachability, but in this problem, we assume a complete graph or use given weights. The intuition is that this provides O(1) access to the distance between any two nodes, which is essential for quickly computing path lengths in the brute-force search. Step-by-step: 1. Let n = number of nodes. 2. Create an n x n matrix graph where graph[i][j] = weight from i to j. 3. For undirected: graph[i][j] = graph[j][i]. 4. Diagonal graph[i][i] = 0 (distance from a node to itself). Time to access: O(1). Space: O(n²). Connection to L3: This matrix is used to compute distances when updating the path cost during DFS, which relies on the bitmask for visited nodes. Common mistakes: Not initializing diagonal to 0, assuming unweighted graph (weights=1), or incorrect indexing. Real code pattern: graph = [[0]*n for _ in range(n)]; then fill based on edges; to get distance from i to j: dist = graph[i][j].

examples.trace: n=3. Initialize 3x3 matrix zeros. Set graph[0][1]=2, graph[1][0]=2. Set graph[0][2]=3, graph[2][0]=3. Set graph[1][2]=4, graph[2][1]=4. Diagonals: graph[0][0]=0, etc.
n=2. Initialize 2x2 zeros. Set graph[0][1]=5, graph[1][0]=5. Diagonals 0.

testCases.input: nodes=4, edges: 0-1 weight 4, 0-2 weight 5, 0-3 weight 6, 1-2 weight 7, 1-3 weight 8, 2-3 weight 9
nodes=2, no edges (use inf for unreachable)
nodes=4, edges: symmetric with random weights

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Recursion and Function Calls
ladders.desc: Understand recursion as a function calling itself to solve smaller subproblems.
ladders.explanation: Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve a problem by breaking it down into smaller, similar subproblems. The intuition is that many problems have a self-similar structure: solving the problem for size n can be reduced to solving it for size n-1, plus some work. In this context, we use recursion in DFS to explore paths: at each step, we call the same function to extend the path further. Key components: 1. Base case: the condition that stops recursion (e.g., all nodes visited). 2. Recursive case: the function calls itself with modified parameters (e.g., adding a node to the path). 3. Progress: each recursive call should move toward the base case (e.g., by increasing the number of visited nodes). Step-by-step: To understand, consider factorial: fact(n) = n * fact(n-1) with base case fact(0)=1. Here, in DFS, we have dfs(current, visited): if all visited, return; else for each next node: dfs(next, visited with next marked). Time and space depend on recursion depth; here, depth up to n. Connection to L4: Recursion uses the adjacency matrix to compute distances at each call. Common mistakes: No base case (infinite recursion), not making progress toward base case, or stack overflow for deep recursion. Real code pattern: def recursive_func(params): if base_case: return result; else: modify params; recursive_func(new_params); restore params if needed.

examples.trace: fact(3): not base case (3>0), so return 3 * fact(2). Call fact(2): return 2 * fact(1). Call fact(1): return 1 * fact(0). Call fact(0): base case, return 1. Unwind: fact(1)=1*1=1; fact(2)=2*1=2; fact(3)=3*2=6.

examples.input: Recursive count of nodes in a path, starting with path [A]
examples.trace: Define count(path): if path empty, return 0; else return 1 + count(path[1:]). For path=[A]: not empty, return 1 + count([]). Call count([]): base case, return 0. So 1+0=1.

testCases.input: recursive depth for n=3 in DFS
base case: all nodes visited in n=2 graph
recursive call with path [X,Y], add Z
infinite recursion if no base case for n=1
simulate dfs call for empty graph n=0

approaches.name: DP with Bitmask and BFS O(n^2 * 2^n)
approaches.summary: Use dynamic programming where dp[mask][i] stores the minimum distance to visit nodes in mask ending at node i, initialized with BFS for single nodes and updated by considering transitions from visited subsets. This leverages bitmasking to represent visited nodes and BFS for shortest paths between nodes, suitable for n up to about 15.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Path Visiting All Nodes with DP and Bitmask
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP with bitmask and BFS to find shortest path visiting all nodes in a graph.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the shortest path that visits every node in an undirected graph at least once, starting from any node. The DP with bitmask and BFS approach solves it efficiently for n ≤ 15. Intuition: Represent visited nodes as a bitmask (integer where bit i = 1 if node i visited). Define dp[mask][i] = minimum distance to visit all nodes in mask ending at node i. Initialize dp[1<<i][i] = 0 for each node i (starting point). Use BFS to precompute shortest distances between all pairs of nodes (dist[i][j]). Then, for each mask from small to large, for each ending node i in mask, try to extend by adding an unvisited node j: dp[mask|(1<<j)][j] = min(dp[mask|(1<<j)][j], dp[mask][i] + dist[i][j]). Answer is min(dp[(1<<n)-1][i]) over all i. Why it works: DP ensures optimal substructure—optimal path to visit mask ending at i can be built from smaller masks. BFS gives exact distances between nodes. Time: O(n² * 2ⁿ) from DP states (n*2ⁿ) times transitions (n). Space: O(n * 2ⁿ) for dp array. Connection: Integrates all lower-level concepts. Common mistakes: Not initializing dp with INF, forgetting to use BFS for distances, incorrect bitmask operations. Real code pattern: Initialize dp with INF, set dp[1<<i][i]=0, compute dist via BFS, iterate masks 1 to (1<<n)-1, for each i in mask, for each j not in mask, update dp[mask|1<<j][j] = min(..., dp[mask][i]+dist[i][j]), return min(dp[(1<<n)-1][i]).

examples.trace: Step 1: BFS distances: dist[0][1]=1, dist[0][2]=1, dist[0][3]=1, others symmetric. Step 2: Initialize dp: dp[0001][0]=0, dp[0010][1]=0, dp[0100][2]=0, dp[1000][3]=0. Step 3: Iterate masks. Mask 0001 (node 0): Try add j=1: dp[0011][1]=min(INF,0+1)=1. Similarly dp[0101][2]=1, dp[1001][3]=1. Mask 0010 (node 1): Add j=0: dp[0011][0]=1. Similarly others. Mask 0011 (nodes 0,1): From dp[0011][0]=1, add j=2: dp[0111][2]=1+1=2. Add j=3: dp[1011][3]=1+1=2. From dp[0011][1]=1, add j=2: dp[0111][2]=min(2,1+2)=2, add j=3: dp[1011][3]=min(2,1+2)=2. Continue until mask 1111: dp[1111][0]=4, dp[1111][1]=4, dp[1111][2]=4, dp[1111][3]=4. Min=4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS for All-Pairs Shortest Distances
ladders.desc: Compute shortest distances between all pairs of nodes in an unweighted graph using BFS.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes dist[i][j] = shortest path length from node i to node j in an undirected graph. Intuition: In an unweighted graph, BFS finds shortest paths from a source to all nodes. Run BFS from each node i to get distances to all j. Step-by-step: For each node i from 0 to n-1: Initialize queue with i, distance array d with INF, d[i]=0. While queue not empty: pop node u, for each neighbor v of u: if d[v] == INF: d[v] = d[u] + 1, push v. Set dist[i][j] = d[j]. Time: O(n*(n+m)) where m is edges—BFS from each node takes O(n+m), total O(n² + n*m). For dense graphs m=O(n²), so O(n³). Space: O(n²) for dist matrix. Connection: These distances are used in DP transitions. Common mistakes: Not resetting distance array for each BFS, using DFS instead of BFS (DFS doesn't guarantee shortest). Real code pattern: dist = [[INF]*n for _ in range(n)]; for i in range(n): queue=[i], d=[INF]*n, d[i]=0; while queue: u=queue.pop(0); for v in graph[u]: if d[v]==INF: d[v]=d[u]+1, queue.append(v); for j in range(n): dist[i][j]=d[j].

examples.trace: From node 0: BFS: d[0]=0, neighbors 1 and 2 set to 1. dist[0]=[0,1,1]. From node 1: BFS: d[1]=0, neighbor 0 set to 1, neighbor 2 set to 1 (via 0 or direct). dist[1]=[1,0,1]. From node 2: BFS: d[2]=0, neighbor 0 set to 1, neighbor 1 set to 1. dist[2]=[1,1,0].

From node 0: BFS: d[0]=0, neighbor 1 set to 1, then from 1 neighbor 2 set to 2. dist[0]=[0,1,2]. From node 1: BFS: d[1]=0, neighbors 0 and 2 set to 1. dist[1]=[1,0,1]. From node 2: BFS: d[2]=0, neighbor 1 set to 1, then from 1 neighbor 0 set to 2. dist[2]=[2,1,0].

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP State Transition with Bitmask
ladders.desc: Dynamic programming to compute minimum distance for visiting subsets of nodes.
ladders.explanation: This is the central DP logic: dp[mask][i] = min distance to visit all nodes in mask ending at node i. Intuition: Break problem into subproblems—optimal path for a subset mask ending at i can be built by taking optimal path for a smaller subset mask' ending at some j, then moving from j to i, where mask = mask' ∪ {i}. Transition: dp[mask][i] = min over all j in mask' (dp[mask'][j] + dist[j][i]), where mask' = mask without i. Initialize dp[1<<i][i] = 0. Iterate masks in increasing order of size to ensure dependencies ready. Why it works: Principle of optimality—if we have optimal path for mask' ending at j, extending to i gives candidate for mask. Time: O(n² * 2ⁿ) from iterating all masks (2ⁿ) and for each, up to n ending nodes and n transitions. Space: O(n * 2ⁿ). Connection: Uses distances from BFS and bitmask operations. Common mistakes: Incorrect mask iteration order, not handling initialization, updating dp in-place causing overwrites. Real code pattern: dp = [[INF]*n for _ in range(1<<n)]; for i in range(n): dp[1<<i][i]=0; for mask in range(1, 1<<n): for i in range(n): if mask & (1<<i): for j in range(n): if mask & (1<<j) and i!=j: dp[mask][i] = min(dp[mask][i], dp[mask^(1<<i)][j] + dist[j][i]).

examples.trace: Initialize dp for single nodes. Mask 0011: i=0: dp[0011][0]=dp[0010][1]+dist[1][0]=0+1=1. i=1: dp[0011][1]=dp[0001][0]+dist[0][1]=0+1=1. Similarly compute other masks. Final mask 1111: dp[1111][0]=4, etc., as in L0 example.

ladders.title: Building Block — Bitmask Representation of Subsets
ladders.desc: Using integers as bitmasks to represent subsets of nodes.
ladders.explanation: Bitmasking is a technique where an integer's bits represent elements in a set. For n elements, use an n-bit integer: bit i (0-indexed) = 1 if element i is in subset, 0 otherwise. Intuition: Allows efficient representation and manipulation of subsets using bitwise operations. Key operations: Set bit i: mask | (1<<i). Clear bit i: mask & ~(1<<i). Check if bit i is set: (mask >> i) & 1 or mask & (1<<i) != 0. Iterate all masks from 0 to (1<<n)-1. Why it works: Each mask uniquely encodes a subset, and bitwise ops are O(1). Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1) per mask. Connection: Used in DP state to represent visited nodes. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in bit shifting, confusing 0-indexing, not using parentheses in bit ops. Real code pattern: n=number of nodes; for mask in range(1<<n): # iterate all subsets; if mask & (1<<i): # check node i in subset; new_mask = mask | (1<<j) # add node j.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Breadth-First Search (BFS) on a Graph
ladders.desc: Traverse a graph level by level to find shortest paths from a source.
ladders.explanation: BFS is a graph traversal algorithm that visits nodes in order of their distance from a starting node. Intuition: Use a queue to explore neighbors first, ensuring shortest path in unweighted graphs. Step-by-step: Start with source node s, mark distance 0, enqueue s. While queue not empty: dequeue node u, for each neighbor v of u: if v not visited, set distance d[v] = d[u] + 1, enqueue v. Time: O(V+E) for adjacency list. Space: O(V) for queue and distance array. Connection: Used to compute distances between nodes. Common mistakes: Using stack instead of queue, not marking visited, infinite loops in cyclic graphs. Real code pattern: from collections import deque; def bfs(graph, start): n=len(graph); dist=[-1]*n; dist[start]=0; q=deque([start]); while q: u=q.popleft(); for v in graph[u]: if dist[v]==-1: dist[v]=dist[u]+1; q.append(v); return dist.

examples.trace: Start: dist[0]=0, queue=[0]. Dequeue 0: neighbors 1 and 2, set dist[1]=1, dist[2]=1, queue=[1,2]. Dequeue 1: neighbor 3, set dist[3]=2, queue=[2,3]. Dequeue 2: neighbor 3 already visited, skip. Dequeue 3: neighbors 1 and 2 visited. Queue empty. Dist=[0,1,1,2].
Start: dist[1]=0, queue=[1]. Dequeue 1: neighbors 0 and 2, set dist[0]=1, dist[2]=1, queue=[0,2]. Dequeue 0: neighbor 1 visited. Dequeue 2: neighbor 1 visited. Queue empty. Dist=[1,0,1].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation and Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Represent a graph using adjacency list for efficient neighbor access.
ladders.explanation: A graph consists of nodes (vertices) and edges connecting them. For algorithm problems, we often use adjacency list representation: an array of lists, where graph[i] is a list of neighbors of node i. Intuition: Efficiently store sparse graphs and iterate neighbors. Step-by-step: Given number of nodes n and edges as pairs (u,v), initialize graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]. For each edge (u,v), add v to graph[u] and u to graph[v] if undirected. Time to build: O(E). Space: O(V+E). Connection: BFS and DP rely on this to access neighbors. Common mistakes: Not adding both directions for undirected graphs, index out of bounds, using adjacency matrix unnecessarily. Real code pattern: n=number of nodes; graph=[[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: graph[u].append(v); graph[v].append(u) # if undirected.

examples.trace: Initialize graph=[[],[],[]]. Edge (0,1): add 1 to graph[0], add 0 to graph[1] → graph=[[1],[0],[]]. Edge (1,2): add 2 to graph[1], add 1 to graph[2] → graph=[[1],[0,2],[1]].

Initialize graph=[[],[],[],[]]. Edge (0,1): add 1 to graph[0], add 0 to graph[1] → graph=[[1],[0],[],[]]. Edge (0,2): add 2 to graph[0], add 0 to graph[2] → graph=[[1,2],[0],[0],[]]. Edge (0,3): add 3 to graph[0], add 0 to graph[3] → graph=[[1,2,3],[0],[0],[0]].
